  "opinion": "As a man in his 90s who has spent most of his life working as a cleaner in Milan, I’ve seen how social media can both connect and confuse. With my bachelor’s degree and a background in learning from my parents’ practical education—my father’s vocational diploma and mother’s upper secondary schooling—I’ve always valued facts and careful thought. Now, at this stage of life, I use social media only a few times a month, so I don’t get overwhelmed by the noise. When I do encounter fake news or misleading posts, I stop and ask myself: *‘What’s the source? Is this something I’d trust from a friend or a trusted organization?’* My religious beliefs in Islam teach me to seek truth and avoid spreading falsehoods, so I check if the content aligns with what I know from reliable sources like local mosques or official government channels. I also rely on my high conscientiousness and openness to think through each post thoroughly. For instance, if I see a post claiming a sudden health crisis in my neighborhood, I don’t share it immediately—I call my local community center or a trusted neighbor to verify. My anxiety sometimes makes me cautious, but that’s why I take time to verify before reacting. Social media can be a tool for good, but it’s important to stay grounded in reality, especially when you’ve lived through so much. The key is to slow down, question, and reach out to those who know the truth.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a man in his 90s who has spent most of his life working as a cleaner in Milan, I’ve seen how social media can both connect and confuse. With my bachelor’s degree and a background in learning from my parents’ practical education—my father’s vocational diploma and mother’s upper secondary schooling—I’ve always valued facts and careful thought. Now, at this stage of life, I use social media only a few times a month, so I don’t get overwhelmed by the noise. When I do encounter fake news or misleading posts, I stop and ask myself: *‘What’s the source? Is this something I’d trust from a friend or a trusted organization?’* My religious beliefs in Islam teach me to seek truth and avoid spreading falsehoods, so I check if the content aligns with what I know from reliable sources like local mosques or official government channels. I also rely on my high conscientiousness and openness to think through each post thoroughly. For instance, if I see a post claiming a sudden health crisis in my neighborhood, I don’t share it immediately—I call my local community center or a trusted neighbor to verify. My anxiety sometimes makes me cautious, but that’s why I take time to verify before reacting. Social media can be a tool for good, but it’s important to stay grounded in reality, especially when you’ve lived through so much. The key is to slow down, question, and reach out to those who know the truth.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 90-year-old Italian man with a bachelor’s degree, anxious, and religiously observant. His background in practical education from his parents and his cautious nature inform his approach to social media. He uses it sparingly, so he avoids the stress of constant engagement. His high conscientiousness and openness drive him to verify information carefully. His Islamic faith emphasizes truthfulness, which shapes his skepticism toward fake content. The opinion focuses on his personal habits: verifying sources, consulting trusted community figures, and using his anxiety as a guide to avoid spreading misinformation. The tone is reflective, calm, and grounded in lived experience.
response_parsed.tone: Reflective, calm, grounded in lived experience
citations: Islamic principles on truth and verification
